text: Olive oil is the principal source of fat in the Mediterranean diet. Extra-virgin olive oil is rich in monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols, which have been associated with reduced cardiovascular risk.

In a randomized controlled trial of 312 adults, daily consumption of walnuts and almonds significantly lowered LDL cholesterol compared with a control diet. Tree nuts appear to improve lipid profiles without promoting weight gain.

Whole grains such as oats, brown rice, and quinoa are important sources of dietary fiber. Higher whole grain intake was inversely associated with the incidence of type 2 diabetes in this prospective cohort.

Leafy green vegetables, including spinach, kale, and broccoli, provide folate, vitamin K, and dietary nitrate. Consumption of these vegetables was linked to lower blood pressure in middle-aged participants.
Fatty fish such as salmon and mackerel deliver long-chain omega-3 fatty acids. Two servings of fish per week is the dietary recommendation for reducing cardiovascular disease risk.
Legumes, including lentils, chickpeas, and soybeans, are a low-cost source of plant protein. Replacing red meat with legumes reduced saturated fat intake in the intervention arm.
High intake of sugar-sweetened beverages was associated with obesity and elevated fasting glucose. The association persisted after adjustment for total energy intake and physical activity.
Iron deficiency anemia remains common among children and pregnant women. Iron-rich foods include red meat, legumes, and fortified cereals, though absorption from plant sources is lower.

Dairy products are a major dietary source of calcium. Milk, yogurt, and cheese contribute roughly two-thirds of calcium intake in Western diets. Fermented dairy such as yogurt may also support gut health.
Fruits such as apples, bananas, oranges, and berries supply vitamin C, potassium, and fiber. A varied intake of fruit is encouraged as part of a balanced diet.
Tofu and other soy foods are staple protein sources in many vegetarian diets. Soy is also a source of isoflavones, plant compounds studied for their effects on bone health.
Saturated fat, found in butter, fatty meat, and coconut oil, raises LDL cholesterol. Replacing saturated fat with unsaturated fat from olive oil or nuts lowers cardiovascular risk.
The dietary guidelines recommend filling half the plate with vegetables and fruit. Choose whole grains over refined grains, and prefer fish, poultry, beans, and nuts as protein sources.
Limit added sugar and salt. Sugar-sweetened drinks and highly processed snack foods should be consumed only occasionally. Drink water instead of soft drinks.
People with a peanut allergy must avoid peanuts and foods processed alongside them. Tree nuts such as almonds and cashews are a separate allergen group and may be tolerated.
A heart-healthy eating pattern emphasizes vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, and olive oil while limiting red meat and processed meat. This pattern resembles the Mediterranean diet.
